food_subgroup: Stuffed tofu and vegetable dish with spicy mala sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general stuffed tofu category; no specific Yong Tau Foo with mala sauce entry exists. FOODON_03311737 (stuffed tofu food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific Yong Tau Foo entry. A0CKP (tofu and soy-based dishes) used as closest parent. Mala sauce component not captured in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Yong Tau Foo or mala-sauced stuffed tofu dishes.

main_ingredients: mala sauce (chilli oil, Sichuan peppercorn, dried chilli, garlic, fermented black bean)

serving.description: 8 pieces of Yong Tau Foo with mala sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving consists of 8 pieces of assorted stuffed tofu and vegetables (approximately 320 g) dressed with mala sauce, typically consumed as a main meal in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Yong Tau Foo (mixed, with sauce) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for Yong Tau Foo (mixed pieces with soup/sauce base). Mala sauce component nutrient contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for chilli oil and Sichuan spice blend. Values represent a weighted composite per 100 g of the assembled dish including sauce. HPB Foodo entry for 'Yong Tau Foo (with sauce)' used as primary anchor; mala sauce adjustment applied to sodium, fat, and energy values based on USDA SR Legacy data for chilli-oil-based sauces.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed tofu and vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on tofu-based mixed dish GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Yong Tau Foo with mala sauce. GI estimated at 40 based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed tofu and vegetable dishes (GI range 35–45, low category). The dominant carbohydrate contributors are tofu (very low GI ~15) and fish paste with minor vegetable components. Mala sauce contributes negligible carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI of approximately 40 is defensible. GL per serving = (40 × 7.5 × 3.2) / 100 = 9.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from mala sauce and fish paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced mala sauce, as a single 320 g serving contributes approximately 1344 mg sodium (58% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by HPB Singapore).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yong Tau Foo with mala sauce has a low GI of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of ~9.6 per serving, making it a suitable meal choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added high-GI noodles or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and chilli oil fat content from mala sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The mala sauce contributes saturated fat and high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a lighter sauce option or reduce mala sauce quantity to keep saturated fat and sodium within cardioprotective dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yong Tau Foo with Mala Sauce (8 pieces) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese dish consisting of eight pieces of assorted tofu, fish paste-stuffed vegetables, and soy-based items — commonly including stuffed bitter gourd, eggplant, lady's finger (okra), tofu puffs, and fish balls — dressed with a mala (麻辣) sauce. Mala sauce is a Sichuan-influenced spicy and numbing condiment made from chilli oil, dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, and fermented black bean paste. The dish is rooted in Hakka Chinese culinary tradition, where 'yong' means 'stuffed' and 'tau foo' means 'tofu'. In Singapore, Yong Tau Foo is a hawker staple typically served with a choice of soup, dry sauce, or mala sauce. The mala variant has grown in popularity since the 2010s, driven by the broader mala trend in Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (~7.2 g per 100 g) from fish paste and tofu, low-to-moderate fat (~4.8 g per 100 g) with the mala sauce contributing chilli oil, and a relatively low carbohydrate content (~7.5 g per 100 g) when consumed without noodles or rice. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it a reasonable meal option for individuals managing blood glucose. The primary clinical concern is sodium, as the mala sauce is salt-rich; a standard 8-piece serving may contribute over 1300 mg sodium. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarians. It is not certified halal due to the use of fermented black bean paste and potential cross-contamination in hawker settings.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for Yong Tau Foo with mala sauce as a combined dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for Yong Tau Foo (mixed pieces with sauce) as the primary anchor, with mala sauce fat and sodium adjustments estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for chilli-oil-based sauces. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database tofu-based mixed dish entries. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA source data for this dish category. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary or secondary sources used for this dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients; no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional mala sauce or fish paste preparation). 4. Nutrient values represent the assembled dish including mala sauce at a typical sauce-to-solid ratio observed in Singapore hawker servings. 5. Halal status is marked false as mala sauce commonly contains fermented black bean paste (may contain alcohol traces) and hawker preparation environments are typically not halal-certified. 6. GI estimated at 40 based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous tofu and vegetable mixed dish entries (GI range 35–45). GL per serving calculated as (40 × 7.5 × 3.2) / 100 = 9.6. 7. Serving size of 320 g is based on a standard 8-piece Yong Tau Foo serving as observed in Singapore hawker and food delivery contexts (approximately 40 g per piece). 8. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for tofu, fish paste, and mixed vegetables and are approximate.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Identified that food_group should be 'Mixed' rather than 'Grains and staples' as the dish is a composite of tofu, fish paste, and vegetables.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback for q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_coverage. The alias 'Yong Tow Foo Ma La' from aliases_by_language.romanized was missing from the flat aliases list. This alias was added to the flat aliases list to ensure perfect consistency as required by the 'Aliases consistency rule'. Confirmed -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are documented in notes. Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is documented as confirmed zero. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (exact, closest_parent, none). Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass.
